1: The Passover (Heb. pesach) became the foundational festival of Israelite identity — the annual re-enactment and remembrance of YHWH's redemptive act. The Passover Seder developed in the Second Temple period and was the meal Jesus shared with his disciples on the night of his arrest. Jesus's identification of the cup with his blood and the bread with his body (Luke 22:19-20) reinterprets the Passover meal through the lens of his approaching death: the new exodus is accomplished through his death as the new Passover lamb. Paul's 'Christ our Passover has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7) is the theological crystallization of this identification.
2: 'This month shall be for you the beginning of months (rosh chadashim). It shall be the first month of the year for you': The Passover month (later called Nisan from the Babylonian month-name Nisannu, or Aviv in the older Hebrew calendar) is established as the liturgical new year. Pre-exilic Israel appears to have observed two calendars: a civil year beginning in autumn (cf. the Gezer Calendar; the Feast of Ingathering 'at the turn of the year,' Exodus 23:16) and this sacred year beginning in spring. The spring new year corresponded to the agricultural cycle of barley planting in Canaan. The Babylonian exile would reinforce the spring new year through exposure to the Babylonian akitu festival (New Year celebration) also held in Nisan.
3: 'Tell all the congregation of Israel (adat Yisrael) that on the tenth day of this month every man shall take a lamb (seh) according to their fathers' houses, a lamb for a household': The structural unit is the household (beit av, father's house) — a multi-generational kinship group, not merely the nuclear family. The tenth of Nisan became known as Lamb Selection Day; in Second Temple period Jerusalem, pilgrims would enter the city with their paschal lamb precisely on the 10th (cf. the chronological structure of the Passion narratives, where Jesus's entry into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday aligns with this day). Seh (H7716) means a young animal of the flock — lamb or kid; v5 specifies kesev o ez (from the sheep or goats).
4: 'And if the household is too small for a lamb, then he and his nearest neighbor shall take according to the number of persons; according to what each can eat you shall make your count for the lamb': The communal sharing of the Passover lamb — sized to the eating capacity of the participating group — established the fundamentally communal character of the meal. The Mishnah later specified that the lamb must be completely consumed: any remainder must be burned, not stored, establishing that the Passover was a feast of complete consumption, not a partial offering. The calculation of peh (mouth, eating-capacity) per lamb formalizes what later became the practice of forming registered chaburot (Passover fellowship groups).
5: 'Your lamb shall be without blemish (tamim), a male a year old. You may take it from the sheep or from the goats': Three criteria: (1) tamim (H8549, without blemish/whole/complete) — the same word used for the holiness standard of priestly qualifications (Leviticus 21:17-21) and for Noah (Genesis 6:9, 'tamim in his generations') and Abraham ('walk before me and be tamim,' Genesis 17:1). (2) Male (zachar), corresponding to the firstborn males who will be spared. (3) ben shanah (year-old) — in its prime, not too old to be eaten or too young to be a full offering. The Septuagint's teleion (perfect) for tamim carries the same semantic range, and Hebrews 9:14 applies the same concept to Christ as the unblemished sacrifice.
6: 'And you shall keep it until the fourteenth day of this month, when the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill their lambs at twilight (bein ha'arbayim)': The four days of keeping the lamb (10th-14th) allowed for inspection to confirm the animal's blemish-free status. Bein ha'arbayim (between the evenings, H6153) — the technical phrase for the late afternoon, roughly 3-6 pm; the Pharisees interpreted this as 3 pm-sunset, the Sadducees as sunset-full dark. The timing corresponds exactly to the hour of the afternoon daily sacrifice in the Temple, and the Synoptic Gospels note that Jesus's crucifixion began at the third hour and he died at the ninth hour (3 pm) — precisely within bein ha'arbayim.
7: 'Then they shall take some of the blood and put it on the two doorposts (mezuzot) and the lintel (mashkof) of the houses in which they eat it': Mezuzah (H4201, doorpost) is the word later used for the container holding the Shema passage affixed to doorposts (Deuteronomy 6:9; Exodus 11:20) — the apotropaic marking of the threshold. The mashkof (H4947, lintel — the horizontal beam over the door) combined with the two doorposts creates a three-point frame. The hyssop application (v22) would have produced a shape roughly corresponding to the three blood-marks of the later described ritual. The threshold-marking with blood has analogs in ancient apotropaic practice across the ANE; what is distinctive here is that it is not to ward off demons but to signal to YHWH a house sheltered under covenant.
8: 'They shall eat the flesh that night, roasted on fire (tseli-esh); with unleavened bread (matzot) and bitter herbs (merorim) they shall eat it': Three prohibitions define the preparation negatively: not raw (na), not boiled in water (mevushal bamayim), but roasted — tseli (H6748) from tsalah (to roast/broil). The prohibition of boiling may have anti-syncretistic intent (some ANE ritual meals involved boiled meat). Matzot (unleavened bread) — the hasty-departure bread that cannot rise. Merorim (bitter herbs, H4844) — the traditional identification includes lettuce, endives, horseradish, and other bitter plants that grew in Sinai; the Passover Seder preserves all three elements in the korech sandwich.
9: 'Do not eat any of it raw or boiled in water, but roasted, its head with its legs and its inner parts': The instruction to roast the lamb whole — rosho al kera'av ve'al kirbo (its head with its legs and with its inner parts) — maintains the integrity of the animal. Whole-animal presentation in ancient sacrificial contexts signified completeness and the offering of the entire life. The prohibition against breaking the animal into parts for distribution connects to v46's prohibition on breaking any bone — the whole lamb must remain structurally intact.
10: 'And you shall let none of it remain until the morning; anything that remains until the morning you shall burn': The prohibition on leftover Passover sacrifice has two practical implications: (1) the meal must be large enough to consume the entire lamb, hence the household-size calculation of v4; (2) no portion may be reserved for later use, separating the Passover from ordinary sacrificial practice where portions could be kept for two days (Leviticus 7:15-16). The burning of remains, rather than their being given to priests or the poor, signals that the Passover is a consumption-meal, not a distribution-ritual.
11: 'In this manner you shall eat it: with your belt fastened (motneikhem chagurim), your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand. And you shall eat it in haste (bechippazon). It is the Passover (pesach) of YHWH': The eating posture is that of a traveler ready to depart: belted, shod, and staffed. Chippaz (H2649, haste, urgency) appears only here and in Deuteronomy 16:3 ('bread of affliction, for you came out of Egypt in haste') and in Isaiah 52:12 ('you shall not go out in haste') — in that latter passage, the second exodus from Babylon will NOT require haste because YHWH goes before and behind. Pesach (H6453) — the word's etymology is debated: it may derive from pasach (to pass over, to spare) or from a root meaning 'to protect.' The narrative in v13 uses pasachti (I will pass over) as a verb; the connection between the noun and verb is explicit in the text even if the etymology is not definitive.
12: 'For I will pass through the land of Egypt that night, and I will strike all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and beast; and on all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments (shefatim): I am YHWH': The explicit statement that the plagues constitute a judicial proceeding (shefatim, H8201, judgments/legal decisions) against the elohei Mitzrayim (gods of Egypt) is the theological center of the plague narrative. Each plague targeted a specific Egyptian deity: the Nile (Hapi, Khnum, Neith), frogs (Heqet), sun (Ra, Amun-Ra), etc. The final plague targets the most sacred of all: the divine kingship. 'I am YHWH' — the self-identification formula (ani YHWH) concludes the judicial declaration, asserting that the one who executes these judgments is identical with the one who made the covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Exodus 6:2-8).
13: 'The blood shall be a sign (ot) for you, on the houses where you are. And when I see the blood, I will pass over (pasachti) you, and no plague will befall you to destroy you, when I strike the land of Egypt': The blood functions as an ot (sign/token) — the same word used for the rainbow covenant with Noah (Genesis 9:12-13), for circumcision (Genesis 17:11), and for the Sabbath (Exodus 31:13). The sign is visible to God, not to the plague-angel — 'when I see the blood' is the divine perspective. The Passover blood's efficacy is entirely dependent on YHWH's seeing and recognizing it; the blood is not an autonomous magical protection but a covenant marker within a personal divine relationship. The verb pasach in 'I will pass over you' (ufasachti) is used in Isaiah 31:5 for YHWH 'hovering over' and 'delivering' Jerusalem — suggesting the root may carry the meaning of protective passing-over, not merely movement past.
14: 'This day shall be for you a memorial (zikkaron) day, and you shall keep it as a feast to YHWH; throughout your generations, as a statute forever, you shall keep it as a feast': Zikkaron (H2146, memorial/remembrance) — the institutional mechanism of ritual memory in Israel's liturgical life. The Passover is explicitly designated as the annual re-enactment that makes each generation a participant in the original exodus: 'In every generation a person is obligated to see himself as though he personally left Egypt' (m. Pesachim Exodus 10:5). The permanent statute language (chukkat olam) places the Passover in the category of the most durable covenantal obligations.
15: 'Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread (matzot). On the first day you shall remove leaven from your houses, for if anyone eats what is leavened from the first day until the seventh day, that person shall be cut off from Israel (venichrat)': The seven days of matzot are a separate festival immediately following Passover night, giving the combined feast the name 'Passover and Feast of Unleavened Bread' in later literature. The removal of all chametz (leaven) from the house became the subject of elaborate Jewish law and the occasion for the night-search (bedikat chametz) on the eve of Passover. Karet (cutting off) for eating leaven is unusually severe for a dietary regulation; its application here confirms that the unleavened-bread requirement is covenantally constitutive, not merely ceremonial.
16: 'On the first day you shall hold a holy assembly (miqra qodesh), and on the seventh day a holy assembly. No work shall be done on those days': Miqra qodesh (H4744+H6944, holy convocation/sacred assembly) — a term used throughout the festival calendar (Lev 23) for days when the community formally assembles before God. The bookending of the seven days with assemblies on the first and seventh days — analogous to the Sabbath structure — embeds the feast of Unleavened Bread within a weekly-rest theology. The exception 'but what everyone needs to eat, that alone may be prepared by you' distinguishes this from the full Sabbath prohibition; domestic food preparation continues.
17: 'And you shall observe the Feast of Unleavened Bread, for on this very day I brought your hosts (tzivotekhem) out of the land of Egypt': Tzivot (H6635, hosts/armies/organized groups) — the same word in 'YHWH Tzvaot' (Lord of Hosts). Here used for Israel's tribal contingents, it frames the exodus as a military march, not a fugitive flight. The imperative 'observe/guard' (shemartem) carries both liturgical (preserve the feast) and security (guard the matzot) meanings — the wordplay that the unleavened bread is both edible and a 'guarded' thing.
18: 'In the first month, from the fourteenth day of the month at evening, you shall eat unleavened bread until the twenty-first day of the month at evening': The precise dates — 14th evening to 21st evening — make the Feast of Unleavened Bread exactly seven days, with Passover night (14th-15th) as the opening event. The evening-to-evening reckoning of the day is the consistent biblical convention, deriving from the 'evening and morning' of Genesis 1. This calendar precision would become the basis for the later Second Temple debates about the exact day of the Passover sacrifice (cf. the Synoptic-Johannine chronological tension in the passion narratives).
19: 'For seven days no leaven shall be found in your houses. If anyone eats what is leavened, that person will be cut off from the congregation of Israel, whether he is a sojourner (ger) or a native of the land (ezrach ha'aretz)': The explicit inclusion of the ger (resident alien/sojourner) under the same obligations as the ezrach (native-born) establishes the principle of one law for all who dwell among Israel (cf. v49). The ger in the Pentateuch is a protected person who has attached themselves to Israel's community without full ethnic membership; their inclusion in the Passover regulation (with circumcision as the qualifying condition, v48) shows the feast's permeability to the nations who align with YHWH.
20: 'You shall eat nothing leavened; in all your dwelling places you shall eat unleavened bread': The spatial universality — 'in all your dwelling places' (bechol moshvoteikhem) — extends the unleavened-bread regulation beyond the Passover meal location to the entire inhabited space of Israel. This was interpreted as meaning that even when traveling, away from home, or in a foreign country, an Israelite could not eat leavened bread during the seven days. Paul uses leaven as a metaphor for sin in 1 Corinthians 5:6-8 explicitly within a Passover framework: 'Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.'
21: Then Moses called all the elders of Israel and said to them, 'Go and select lambs for yourselves according to your clans, and kill the Passover lamb': Moses's transmission of the Passover instructions to the elders (zeqenim) — the tribal and clan leadership — is the structure through which the feast is communicated to all of Israel. The elders are responsible for their clans' compliance; the Passover is both a national and a family-unit observance simultaneously. The verb mishchu (select, draw out) — from mashach (to draw/pull) — is the same root as Moses's name (Exodus 2:10, 'meshitihu from the water') and incidentally of mashiach (the anointed one drawn out); the layers of naming in Exodus are rarely accidental.
22: 'Take a bunch of hyssop (aguddat ezov), dip it in the blood that is in the basin (bassaf), and touch the lintel and the two doorposts with the blood that is in the basin': Ezov (H231, hyssop — likely Origanum syriacum, Syrian marjoram, a small bushy plant with absorbent flower clusters that makes an effective brush) is used consistently in biblical purity rituals: for skin-disease cleansing (Leviticus 14:4,6,49-52), for the red heifer ash-water (Numbers 19:6,18), and in Psalms 51:7 ('purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean'). Its use here as the applicator for protective blood marks it as a ritual-purity instrument even in the proto-priestly context of the first Passover. The saf (threshold/basin) is interpreted by some as a basin at the door's base; others read it as the threshold itself, making the blood frame around the entire doorway.
23: 'For YHWH will pass through to strike Egypt, and when he sees the blood on the lintel and on the two doorposts, YHWH will pass over the door and will not allow the destroyer (hamashchit) to enter your houses to strike you': Here a new agent appears: hamashchit (the Destroyer, H7843) — a figure distinct from YHWH who 'will not allow' the destroyer to enter. The Destroyer is the executive agent of the judgment while YHWH remains the authorizing agent. The 'angel of YHWH' executing judgment appears in 2 Samuel 24:16 (the plague after David's census), 2 Kings 19:35 (the Assyrian army), and 1 Chronicles 21:15. Hebrews 11:28 specifically notes that Moses kept the Passover 'so that the Destroyer of the firstborn might not touch them' — confirming this Destroyer reading.
24: 'You shall observe this rite as a statute for you and for your sons forever': The permanent statute language (chok lecha ulevanecha ad olam) frames the Passover as a constitutional ordinance in Israel's covenantal law — not a temporary emergency measure but an eternal institution. The annual Passover Seder in Jewish observance today — unchanged in its basic structure for over two millennia — is the direct institutional continuation of this verse's mandate.
25: 'And when you come to the land that YHWH will give you, as he has promised, you shall keep this service': The future orientation — 'when you come to the land' — embeds the Passover in the larger narrative of the Abrahamic promise. The feast is not only about what God did in Egypt but about the trajectory toward the inheritance. Deuteronomy 16 revises the original Passover instructions to centralize the feast at the one sanctuary once Israel is settled — a development that reflects the changing social-geographic reality of a settled people versus a wandering people.
26: 'And when your children say to you, What do you mean by this service?': The catechetical question assumes an observant household where the annual ritual is visible and curious to children who were not present at the original event. The four questions of the Passover Haggadah are a direct institutional development of this verse; the Seder was explicitly designed as a pedagogical occasion where children's questions generate the narrative retelling. The three Passover questions in Exodus 12:26-27, Exodus 13:14-15, and Deuteronomy 6:20-21 each receive different answers depending on the asking child's implied sophistication — a distinction that becomes the Haggadah's four-children paradigm.
27: 'You shall say: It is the sacrifice of YHWH's Passover, for he passed over the houses of the people of Israel in Egypt, when he struck the Egyptians but spared our houses. And the people bowed their heads and worshiped': The narrative answer — historical event, specific God, specific action — is the paradigm for all subsequent Jewish historical theology: the covenant relationship is established and maintained through the recitation of what YHWH actually did. Vayiqod ha'am vayishtachu (the people bowed and worshiped) — the prostration vocabulary of covenant ratification, the same response as at Exodus 4:31 when they believed Moses's announcement of deliverance.
28: Then the people of Israel went and did so; as YHWH had commanded Moses and Aaron, so they did: The compliance formula closes the instruction-section. In the Tabernacle narrative (Exod 39-40) this formula will be repeated eight times to establish that Israel's construction perfectly matched the divine specifications. Here it marks the transition from instruction to execution: the Passover was not only prescribed but obeyed.
29: At midnight YHWH struck all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon, and all the firstborn of the livestock: The execution matches the announcement of Exodus 11:4-5 precisely. The social range — from the throne to the dungeon — is the maximum possible sweep, encompassing all of Egyptian society. In Egyptian legal practice, even prisoners in the dungeon were Egyptian subjects with legally defined status; the plague spares no Egyptian institutional structure. The livestock firstborn are included — the fifth plague (Exodus 9:3-6) had already killed the livestock once; their firstborn now die again — a cumulative demonstration that YHWH's sovereignty extends to every domain of Egyptian life.
30: And Pharaoh rose up in the night, he and all his servants and all the Egyptians. And there was a great cry in Egypt, for there was not a house where someone was not dead: The tze'aqah gedolah (great cry) matches the prediction of 11:6 exactly. The formula 'not a house where someone was not dead' (ein bayit asher ein sham met) is the inverse of 'not a dog shall growl against Israel' (Exodus 11:7) — the silence of Israel's deliverance is matched by the universal wail of Egypt's judgment. This cry answers and mirrors Israel's original cry of slavery (Exodus 2:23-24, vayizaqu) — the theological symmetry of the narrative is precisely constructed.
31: Then he summoned Moses and Aaron by night and said, 'Up, go out from among my people, both you and the people of Israel; and go, serve YHWH, as you have said': The verb vayiqra (he summoned) followed by Pharaoh addressing Moses directly — in the middle of the night, the king who threatened Moses with death (Exodus 10:28) sends for him with urgency. The fourfold imperative — 'rise up, go out, serve YHWH' plus 'take your flocks and your herds' (v32) — is Pharaoh's unconditional surrender. The conditions he had tried to maintain throughout the plague negotiations (men only, adults only, no livestock) are all abandoned. The political capitulation is total.
32: 'Take your flocks and your herds, as you have said, and be gone, and bless me also!': The request 'bless me also' (uveirakhtem gam oti) — Pharaoh asking Israel's blessing — is one of the most remarkable reversals in the narrative. The oppressor asks the blessing of the oppressed. It may reflect genuine terror (perhaps YHWH's blessing on departing Israel might protect Egypt from further judgment) or it may be a conventional departure formula; in either reading it demonstrates the reversal of relational status. Egypt's king, who held Israel's life in his hands, now solicits Israel's intercession.
33: The Egyptians were urgent with the people to send them out of the land in haste. For they said, 'We shall all be dead': The Egyptian fear-statement 'kullanu metim' (we are all dead men) is hyperbolic but psychologically credible: in a land where every family has lost a firstborn, the terror of what further refusal might bring is absolute. The Egyptians' urgency to expel Israel contrasts with their previous resistance to release; the same population that oppressed Israel now pushes them out. This reversal fulfills the prophetic statement of 3:21 that Egypt would give the Israelites what they asked.
34: So the people took their dough before it was leavened, their kneading bowls being bound up in their cloaks on their shoulders: The hasty departure in v34 is the narrative explanation for the unleavened bread of the feast: there was literally no time for the dough to rise. The mishearotam (kneading bowls) carried on shoulders in cloaks provides a vivid physical detail that anchors the liturgical requirement in a specific historical moment. The Passover Haggadah quotes this verse as the original context for the matzah eaten at the Seder.
35: The people of Israel had also done as Moses told them, for they had asked the Egyptians for silver and gold jewelry and for clothing: The compliance with Moses's earlier instruction (Exodus 11:2) is confirmed. The material transfer represents the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 15:14, 'they shall come out with great possessions') and functions as back-wages for generations of unpaid slave labor. The spoiling of Egypt was not theft but restitution within YHWH's covenantal economy.
36: YHWH had given the people favor (chen) in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they let them have what they asked. Thus they plundered the Egyptians (vanatslu et Mitzrayim): Natzal (H5337, to rescue, to strip, to plunder) — the verb of the spoiling of Egypt is the same root used for YHWH 'delivering' Israel from Pharaoh's hand (Exodus 3:8, vahatzilticha). The plundering of Egypt and the deliverance of Israel are linguistically the same act; Israel is 'rescued/stripped from' Egypt even as Egypt is 'stripped' by Israel. The chen (favor) that moved the Egyptians to give is YHWH's sovereign disposition of the human heart — the same mechanism as Pharaoh's hardening, but in the opposite direction.
37: And the people of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand men on foot (regali), besides women and children: The first geographical waypoint: from Ra'amses (identified with modern Qantir/Tell el-Dab'a, the site of Ramesses II's Delta capital Pi-Ramesses, extensive excavations of which have confirmed large-scale construction activity in the period) to Succoth (possibly Tell el-Maskhuta in the eastern Delta). The figure 600,000 men (shesh-me'ot elef ragli) — with women and children suggesting a total of 2-3 million — has generated extensive scholarly debate. Elef (H505) can mean 'thousand' or 'clan unit/contingent'; some scholars read the 600 contingents as approximately 5,550 men, yielding a total of ~20,000 — a number consistent with Egyptian New Kingdom records of Asiatic settlements in the Delta. The canonical reading of 600,000 is the dominant Jewish and Christian tradition.
38: A mixed multitude (erev rav) also went up with them, and very much livestock, both flocks and herds: Erev rav (H6153+H7227, mixed/great mixture) — the phrase is unique to this verse and Nehemiah 13:3. It designates a non-Israelite group accompanying the exodus: Egyptians, Canaanite slaves in Egypt, other Semites, or a mix of all these. The inclusion of the erev rav has been variously interpreted in later tradition: Philo sees them as Egyptian converts; rabbinic tradition sometimes blames them for the golden calf (the 'mixed multitude' who brought idolatry). Theologically, their presence signals that the exodus was not an ethnically closed event — others joined in the departure from Egypt's system.
39: And they baked unleavened cakes (ugot matzot) of the dough that they had brought out of Egypt, for it was not leavened, because they were thrust out of Egypt and could not wait, nor had they prepared any provisions for themselves: The narrative circles back to v34 (the unbaked dough) to explain the matzot. Ugot (H5692, round flat cakes) — the shape of the baked matzah. 'They could not wait' (lo yakhelu lehitmahmeah) and 'had prepared no provisions' (lo asu lahem tzedah) are the two reasons for unleavened bread: urgency prevented rising, and pre-planning was impossible. Both the feast and the liturgy are rooted in a specific historical emergency, not merely a symbolic choice.
40: The time that the people of Israel lived in Egypt was 430 years: The MT reading of 430 years applies the whole period to Egypt; the LXX adds 'and in Canaan' (430 years counting from Abraham's sojourn in Canaan, Gen 12, through the exodus, giving approximately 215 years in Egypt proper). Paul's chronology in Galatians 3:17 ('the law, which came 430 years afterwards, does not annul a covenant previously ratified by God') uses the LXX counting from the Abrahamic covenant, not from the descent into Egypt. The MT 430 years is consistent with Egyptian chronologies if the descent occurred in the Middle Kingdom Hyksos period (c. 1650 BC) and the exodus in the early New Kingdom (c. 1270-1250 BC). 1 Kings 6:1's '480 years from the exodus to Solomon's fourth year' supports a 15th-century date for the exodus if taken literally.
41: At the end of 430 years, on that very day (be'etzem hayom hazeh), all the hosts of YHWH went out from the land of Egypt: Be'etzem hayom hazeh (on this very day/on the bone of this day) is a phrase of exact chronological precision used for dramatic moments: Noah's flood (Genesis 7:13), the giving of circumcision to Abraham (Genesis 17:23), and the death of Moses on Pisgah (Deuteronomy 32:48). The phrase marks the exodus as a precisely dated, non-ambiguous historical event in YHWH's covenantal calendar. 'All the hosts of YHWH (kol-tzivot YHWH)' — Israel is here called YHWH's armies, the martial framing of the nation that dominates Numbers.
42: It was a night of watching (leil shimmurim) by YHWH, to bring them out of the land of Egypt; so this same night is a night of watching kept for YHWH by all the people of Israel throughout their generations: Leil shimmurim (H3915+H8107, night of watchings/vigil) — the night YHWH himself 'watched over' Israel while executing judgment in Egypt. The reflexive: Israel keeps an annual vigil (shimmurim) in memory of the night YHWH himself kept vigil. The liturgical response mirrors the original divine action. In Second Temple practice, this 'night of watching' became associated with special alertness and prayer during the Passover night.
43: 'This is the statute of the Passover: no foreigner (ben-nekar) shall eat of it': The regulations in vv43-49 address the participation boundaries of the Passover in the new social reality of Israel as a nation with resident aliens, purchased servants, and sojourners. Ben-nekar (H5236+H5237, son of a foreigner — one who maintains alien religious identity, as distinct from the ger who has attached to Israel) is explicitly excluded. The boundary is not ethnic but covenantal — the outsider who has not joined the covenant community cannot participate in the covenant meal.
44: 'Every slave that is bought for money may eat of it after you have circumcised him': A purchased slave — who by acquisition has entered the household of Israel — may eat the Passover after circumcision. The circumcision requirement brings him into the Abrahamic covenant; once covenantally marked, he participates in the covenant feast. The economic and social integration of the slave into the household correlates with covenantal inclusion — ownership confers a form of membership that obligates circumcision.
45: 'No foreigner or hired worker (toshav vesakhir) shall eat of it': Toshav (H8453, resident/sojourner who maintains independence from Israel's household) and sakhir (H7916, hired worker/day laborer) are both excluded — their relationship to Israel is economic and temporary rather than household-covenantal. The Passover is a family meal, not a public feast; casual economic association does not create the social intimacy of the covenant household that the Passover enacts and celebrates.
46: 'It shall be eaten in one house; you shall not take any of the flesh outside the house, and you shall not break any of its bones (ve'etsem lo tishberu vo)': The two regulations — in one house and no broken bones — are the most widely cited of the Passover rules in later Jewish and Christian interpretation. (1) The single-house rule keeps the Passover a household-unit meal, not a communal distribution. (2) 'No bone shall be broken' — etsem (H6106, bone) is the same word used for the 'very day' of v40-41 (be'etzem) — bones represent structural integrity. The prohibition on breaking bones preserves the wholeness of the sacrificial animal. John 19:36 applies this verse directly to Jesus: 'these things took place that the Scripture might be fulfilled: Not one of his bones will be broken.' It is one of the most explicit Passover typological fulfillments in the NT.
47: 'All the congregation of Israel shall keep it': The communal obligation — 'all the congregation' (kol adat Yisrael) — is the corporate dimension of a feast that is simultaneously household-based (family unit) and national (all Israel). The tension between the family-meal character and the national obligation will resolve, in Deuteronomy 16, into the centralized Passover at the national sanctuary, where the national gathering is maintained while the family-unit character of the meal is adapted.
48: 'If a stranger (ger) shall sojourn with you and would keep the Passover to YHWH, let all his males be circumcised. Then he may come near and keep it; he shall be as a native of the land (ke'ezrach ha'aretz). But no uncircumcised person shall eat of it': The ger who wants to participate undergoes the full covenant sign — male circumcision for the entire household — and then becomes, for the purpose of the feast, equivalent to a native-born Israelite. This is the most explicit Torah statement of a conversion pathway: covenantal sign + Passover participation = covenantal membership. The Galatian controversy Paul addresses (Galatians 5:2-6) touches directly on whether circumcision remains the covenant entrance requirement in the new covenant.
49: 'There shall be one law (torah achat) for the native and for the stranger who sojourns among you': Torah achat (one law/instruction) — the single standard applying equally to native-born and resident alien is one of the most radical egalitarian principles in the ancient world. Most ancient legal systems had differential treatment for citizens vs. non-citizens; Israel's Passover law explicitly applies one standard. The universal application is repeated in Numbers 9:14 (for the sojourner keeping the Passover), Numbers 15:15-16 (for offerings), and Numbers 15:29 (for inadvertent sin). This principle becomes the basis for Paul's argument that in Christ 'there is neither Jew nor Greek' (Galatians 3:28).
50: All the people of Israel did just as YHWH commanded Moses and Aaron; so they did: The compliance formula brackets the Passover legislation as faithfully executed. The double confirmation 'so they did' (ken asu) — slightly redundant, which is the point — emphasizes the totality and exactness of Israel's obedience. The Passover generation, who will later be characterized as faithless in the wilderness, are here exemplars of obedience. The tension between this moment and the golden calf episode (Exodus 32) is the narrative engine of the rest of Exodus.
51: And on that very day YHWH brought the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their hosts: Be'etzem hayom hazeh (on that very day) — the same formula as v41, double-confirming the precision. Al-tzivotam (by their hosts/contingents) — Israel exits not as a refugee mob but as an ordered military force. The chapter that opens with the institution of a liturgical calendar and a redemptive meal closes with the march of YHWH's armies. Passover and exodus, feast and freedom, liturgy and history are inseparable from the beginning: Israel's identity is formed at the table and enacted on the road.
